title: From Self To Others
study: A Study In The Life Of Moses
scriptureForWeek: VARIOUS PASSAGES IN EXODUS Make sure you focus on the verses for each day, as these important events are taken from several readings of scripture across several chapters. These are chosen to give an overview of the story of Moses leading the children of Israel out of Egypt.
weekTheme: Boys hesitate, Men act The ultimate goal of living for others is so they can experience freedom. Jesus came to set us free, and he is opposed to everything that enslaves us. But it can be hard to believe that God can do this. This week, we are going to see God step in to bring freedom to the children of Israel. There will be fireworks; there will be blood. There will be angels and agony, death and glory. It's intense. There are several important lessons here to remember as you move into manhood. Freedom never comes without a fight. People who have been enslaved need to learn how to be free. Leaders can wrestle with insecurity even when doing amazing things. It also gives us lots of leadership lessons on how to help people when they deal with fear and anxiety. It’s a strong week, so let’s jump in.

days.title: Focus On Fighting Yourself
days.scripture: LUKE 14:25-33 Large crowds were traveling with Jesus, and turning to them he said: “If anyone comes to me and does not hate father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters—yes, even their own life—such a person cannot be my disciple. And whoever does not carry their cross and follow me cannot be my disciple. “Suppose one of you wants to build a tower. Won’t you first sit down and estimate the cost to see if you have enough money to complete it? For if you lay the foundation and are not able to finish it, everyone who sees it will ridicule you, saying, ‘This person began to build and wasn’t able to finish.’ “Or suppose a king is about to go to war against another king. Won’t he first sit down and consider whether he is able with ten thousand men to oppose the one coming against him with twenty thousand? If he is not able, he will send a delegation while the other is still a long way off and will ask for terms of peace. In the same way, those of you who do not give up everything you have cannot be my disciples.
days.reflection: Let’s pause for a minute and do a halftime recap. You are halfway through the second shift, From Self to Others. Boys live for themselves; men live for others. This is a shift following the life of Moses where he moves from living for himself to living to see the children of Israel set free. Have you sensed these sessions getting a bit more difficult? Have you sensed these sessions getting a bit harder to understand? Have you sensed these sessions being a bit more challenging? If you have felt that way at all, YOU’RE RIGHT! It is hard to live for other people. It’s much easier to just live for yourself. It's way easier to just worry about what you think, what you want, and what matters to you. Other people’s needs make your life more complicated. They make your life uncomfortable; you get dragged into their drama. Making the shift from self to others is probably the hardest of all the shifts. It’s hard because we have a sinful nature that only wants to think about ourselves. It’s hard because you live in a world that constantly tells you how important you are. It’s hard because you live in a world where you can customize all your preferences around yourself and ignore others. So moving from self to others is a struggle. It’s a fight. It’s a wrestling match. It’s a war. Jesus used really strong language to talk about how hard this shift would be. He said we have to “deny ourselves, take up our cross and follow him.” A cross was an implement of death. Jesus knew you had to kill selfishness, not play games with it. The Apostle Paul wrote in 1 Corinthians 15:31 that he “died daily.” He knew it was a constant fight to resist selfishness and love others. If you don’t make this shift, you will end up being miserable. You will end up being a shallow, lonely guy. You don’t want that. No woman wants to marry a selfish man. No employer wants to hire a selfish man. No child wants a selfish dad. No guy wants a selfish friend. God won’t use a selfish man. So if all of this feels hard, a bit boring, or too challenging, hang in there. You are starting to understand the reality and challenges you are up against. But take heart, though this is hard, it’s worth it. Deep in your spirit, you do want to learn to live for others. At the deepest part of who you are, you know you were born for more than simply getting what you want. God is looking to use men who care about others. Women long for men who care about others. Children want to be raised by dads who care for others. You want to be a man who makes a difference for others. So keep fighting, pressing in, showing up, and grinding it out. There is deep joy in making progress in this shift, even if you can’t see it at the moment. Men aren’t made in a moment; they are made in the daily struggle of self-denial. What makes Jesus so compelling and a man worth following was his love for others and willingness to sacrifice himself for them. It will be the same for you. Reflect on this, and tomorrow we will get back to Moses and the most dramatic moment in the story of Israel’s deliverance.
questions: What thoughts or feelings did this little halftime moment bring up in you?
In what ways do you see selfishness promoted as a good thing by culture?
Are there any places in your life where you see yourself struggling with selfishness? What are they? How do they show up?
Ask the person you’re doing this study with to share a time they had to wrestle with selfishness and what they did to try and overcome it.

thoughts.verse: However, I consider my life worth nothing to me; my only aim is to finish the race and complete the task the Lord Jesus has given me —the task of testifying to the good news of God’s grace.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a vision of becoming like Jesus and living for others. Ask him to show you the joy and reward of this and the kind of man you could become.

days.title: Obedience Brings Freedom
days.scripture: EXODUS 12:1-30 While the Israelites were still in the land of Egypt, the LORD gave the following instructions to Moses and Aaron: “From now on, this month will be the first month of the year for you. Announce to the whole community of Israel that on the tenth day of this month each family must choose a lamb or a young goat for a sacrifice, one animal for each household. If a family is too small to eat a whole animal, let them share with another family in the neighborhood. Divide the animal according to the size of each family and how much they can eat. The animal you select must be a one-year-old male, either a sheep or a goat, with no defects. “Take special care of this chosen animal until the evening of the fourteenth day of this first month. Then the whole assembly of the community of Israel must slaughter their lamb or young goat at twilight. They are to take some of the blood and smear it on the sides and top of the doorframes of the houses where they eat the animal. That same night they must roast the meat over a fire and eat it along with bitter salad greens and bread made without yeast. Do not eat any of the meat raw or boiled in water. The whole animal— including the head, legs, and internal organs—must be roasted over a fire. Do not leave any of it until the next morning. Burn whatever is not eaten before morning. “These are your instructions for eating this meal: Be fully dressed, wear your sandals, and carry your walking stick in your hand. Eat the meal with urgency, for this is the LORD’s Passover. On that night I will pass through the land of Egypt and strike down every firstborn son and firstborn male animal in the land of Egypt. I will execute judgment against all the gods of Egypt, for I am the LORD! But the blood on your doorposts will serve as a sign, marking the houses where you are staying. When I see the blood, I will pass over you. This plague of death will not touch you when I strike the land of Egypt. “This is a day to remember. Each year, from generation to generation, you must celebrate it as a special festival to the LORD. This is a law for all time. For seven days the bread you eat must be made without yeast. On the first day of the festival, remove every trace of yeast from your homes. Anyone who eats bread made with yeast during the seven days of the festival will be cut off from the community of Israel. On the first day of the festival and again on the seventh day, all the people must observe an official day for holy assembly. No work of any kind may be done on these days except in the preparation of food. “Celebrate this Festival of Unleavened Bread, for it will remind you that I brought your forces out of the land of Egypt on this very day. This festival will be a permanent law for you; celebrate this day from generation to generation. The bread you eat must be made without yeast from the evening of the fourteenth day of the first month until the evening of the twenty-first day of that month. During those seven days, there must be no trace of yeast in your homes. Anyone who eats anything made with yeast during this week will be cut off from the community of Israel. These regulations apply both to the foreigners living among you and to the native-born Israelites. During those days you must not eat anything made with yeast. Wherever you live, eat only bread made without yeast.” Then Moses called all the elders of Israel together and said to them, “Go, pick out a lamb or young goat for each of your families, and slaughter the Passover animal. Drain the blood into a basin. Then take a bundle of hyssop branches and dip it into the blood. Brush the hyssop across the top and sides of the doorframes of your houses. And no one may go out through the door until morning. For the LORD will pass through the land to strike down the Egyptians. But when he sees the blood on the top and sides of the doorframe, the LORD will pass over your home. He will not permit his death angel to enter your house and strike you down. “Remember, these instructions are a permanent law that you and your descendants must observe forever. When you enter the land the LORD has promised to give you, you will continue to observe this ceremony. Then your children will ask, ‘What does this ceremony mean?’ And you will reply, ‘It is the Passover sacrifice to the LORD, for he passed over the houses of the Israelites in Egypt. And though he struck the Egyptians, he spared our families.’” When Moses had finished speaking, all the people bowed down to the ground and worshiped. So the people of Israel did just as the LORD had commanded through Moses and Aaron. And that night at midnight, the LORD struck down all the firstborn sons in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn son of Pharaoh, who sat on his throne, to the firstborn son of the prisoner in the dungeon. Even the firstborn of their livestock were killed. Pharaoh and all his officials and all the people of Egypt woke up during the night, and loud wailing was heard throughout the land of Egypt. There was not a single house where someone had not died.
days.reflection: What is the first thing that comes to your mind when you think of freedom? For most people, it's freedom to do what they want. Freedom from rules, from authority, free to be who you want. But freedom that is the best for us is freedom to do what God wants. If you are going to learn to shift from thinking about yourself to leading others, you are going to need to learn about the right kind of freedom. The children of Israel are about to experience an epic moment of freedom. God is going to act on their behalf, but first, he wants them to prepare. So he gives Moses clear instructions to get the people ready. This is important to note because people need leaders to tell them what to do, and how to go about doing it. God gave Moses 3 tasks. 1- Help the children of Israel be loyal to God. 24 “Remember, these instructions are a permanent law that you and your descendants must observe forever. The children of Israel hated slavery, but that didn’t necessarily mean they loved Yahweh. They had to choose to leave Egypt and become the people of God. Moses reminded them why all the hardship would be worth it. 2- Prepare the children of Israel to leave. 11 “These are your instructions for eating this meal: Be fully dressed, wear your sandals, and carry your walking stick in your hand. Eat the meal with urgency, for this is the LORD’s Passover. Sometimes when people come out of serious struggles, they don’t know what to do or how to do it. God called Moses to give the people very specific instructions so they could roll when the time was right. They needed direction for their deliverance. 3- Help the children of Israel obey God. When Moses had finished speaking, all the people bowed down to the ground and worshiped. So the people of Israel did just as the LORD had commanded through Moses and Aaron. As you learned earlier, partial obedience is no obedience at all. Moses had to urge the people to obey all the commands that they were given. God wanted his people free from the false ideas and gods of Egypt. I know that when you read all these commands, they can seem super weird. Blood on door posts, bread without yeast, leaving in the middle of the night. But they are actually really symbolic and filled with meaning you will see a bit later. Leading others is real work. If you want people to follow you to freedom, you have to be clear, compelling, and call people back to God. You may have friends who struggle with their faith, or keep giving in to temptation, or feel stuck in their sins. They are going to need help to get free. God used Moses to get his people ready, and he can use you as well. Lead them to the right kind of freedom that actually helps.
questions: What do you think the main difference is between the world’s definition of freedom and God's? Why do you think it could be important to know the difference?
Do you think God's commands are easy or hard to understand? Why or why not? Are there any, in particular, you struggle with? Why?
Have you ever done a “Passover Seder” at church or with friends? If not, ask the person you are doing this study with what they are, and what they can teach us.

thoughts.verse: You, my brothers and sisters, were called to be free. But do not use your freedom to indulge the flesh; rather, serve one another humbly in love. For the entire law is fulfilled in keeping this one command: “Love your neighbor as yourself.”
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you the courage and clarity to help others find freedom. Pray for anyone you know who may feel trapped or stuck in something right now.

days.title: A Dead Heart
days.scripture: EXODUS 11:1-10 - 12:31-42 Then the LORD said to Moses, “I will strike Pharaoh and the land of Egypt with one more blow. After that, Pharaoh will let you leave this country. In fact, he will be so eager to get rid of you that he will force you all to leave. Tell all the Israelite men and women to ask their Egyptian neighbors for articles of silver and gold.” (Now the LORD had caused the Egyptians to look favorably on the people of Israel. And Moses was considered a very great man in the land of Egypt, respected by Pharaoh’s officials and the Egyptian people alike.) Moses had announced to Pharaoh, “This is what the LORD says: At midnight tonight I will pass through the heart of Egypt. All the firstborn sons will die in every family in Egypt, from the oldest son of Pharaoh, who sits on his throne, to the oldest son of his lowliest servant girl who grinds the flour. Even the firstborn of all the livestock will die. 6 Then a loud wail will rise throughout the land of Egypt, a wail like no one has heard before or will ever hear again. But among the Israelites it will be so peaceful that not even a dog will bark. Then you will know that the LORD makes a distinction between the Egyptians and the Israelites. All the officials of Egypt will run to me and fall to the ground before me. ‘Please leave!’ they will beg. ‘Hurry! And take all your followers with you.’ Only then will I go!” Then, burning with anger, Moses left Pharaoh. Now the LORD had told Moses earlier, “Pharaoh will not listen to you, but then I will do even more mighty miracles in the land of Egypt.” Moses and Aaron performed these miracles in Pharaoh’s presence, but the LORD hardened Pharaoh’s heart, and he wouldn’t let the Israelites leave the country. Chapter 12. Pharaoh sent for Moses and Aaron during the night. “Get out!” he ordered. “Leave my people—and take the rest of the Israelites with you! Go and worship the LORD as you have requested. Take your flocks and herds, as you said, and be gone. Go, but bless me as you leave.” All the Egyptians urged the people of Israel to get out of the land as quickly as possible, for they thought, “We will all die!” The Israelites took their bread dough before yeast was added. They wrapped their kneading boards in their cloaks and carried them on their shoulders. And the people of Israel did as Moses had instructed; they asked the Egyptians for clothing and articles of silver and gold. The LORD caused the Egyptians to look favorably on the Israelites, and they gave the Israelites whatever they asked for. So they stripped the Egyptians of their wealth! That night the people of Israel left Rameses and started for Succoth. There were about 600,000 men, plus all the women and children. A rabble of non- Israelites went with them, along with great flocks and herds of livestock. For bread they baked flat cakes from the dough without yeast they had brought from Egypt. It was made without yeast because the people were driven out of Egypt in such a hurry that they had no time to prepare the bread or other food. The people of Israel had lived in Egypt for 430 years. In fact, it was on the last day of the 430th year that all the LORD’s forces left the land. On this night the LORD kept his promise to bring his people out of the land of Egypt. So this night belongs to him, and it must be commemorated every year by all the Israelites, from generation to generation.
days.reflection: Quick quiz: Which world leader gave the following speech? Never, never, never give up. Do you know who said those famous lines? Do you know the situation he was facing? It was Winston Churchill during WW2 when they were facing the might and power of the Nazis. And as history bears out, his perseverance paid off. England and the allies defeated the Nazis, even though Hitler was stubborn and it took a number of years. God will eventually come through, and when he does, it’s going to be epic. So never, never, give up. God's plan to set his people free included his victory and judgment over the gods of Egypt. God says here “I will execute judgment against all the gods of Egypt, for I am the LORD!” God has judged a host of gods, the god of livestock, the Nile, the weather, and even the sun. But now we have the final showdown: God judging Pharaoh. In Pharaoh’s day, he was actually considered to be a god. He believed that he was the most powerful man in all of Egypt. Matthews, V. H., Chavalas notes, “In Egypt Pharaoh was also considered a deity, and this last plague is directed at him. In the ninth plague his “father,” the sun god, was defeated, and now his son, presumably the heir to the throne, will be slaughtered. This is a blow to Pharaoh’s person, his kingship and his divinity.” Even though massive damages had been inflicted on Egypt, Pharaoh refused to let God's people go. His hardness of heart cost him more than he could imagine. This passage teaches you some powerful lessons about helping others. You have to have the courage to warn people that God does not mess around. In our world today, people take God lightly. They use his name as a swear word, mock him, and act like he doesn’t care. God is patient and kind and will give people every change they need, but eventually, he will reach a point where he steps in and acts. Though it can be hard to step up and tell people the truth, and you may be worried about sounding judgmental, you have to speak truth to power when God calls you to. You have to help people ask for the impossible. God gave the Israelites favor in the eyes of the Egyptians, and Moses instructs them to ask for silver and gold. God was helping the Israelites get some resources to start their new lives. They were moving from slavery to freedom, and would need money to establish their future. The people may have thought this was crazy, but God gave them favor in the eyes of the Egyptians. God may call you to ask for crazy impossible things, but always obey him when he does. You have to remind people of the faithfulness of God. Real freedom can take time. Lots of time. For the Israelites, it was 430 years. Think about that!!! Joseph was taken to Egypt 400 years earlier, and at last, God set his people free. But when the timing was right, God was faithful. “On this night the LORD kept his promise to bring his people out of the land of Egypt.” People often get discouraged when things take longer than they hope. One of the roles you will have to play in leading others to freedom is that of being an encourager. When people lose heart, you have to stir their hearts. When people get beat down, you have to lift them up. What a crazy journey Moses has had so far. He has been prepared by God in the palace, formed by God in the desert, and confronted Pharaoh in Egypt. At last, it looks like his vision from all those years ago has come to pass. Pharaoh is defeated, and the people are free. What a relief! Or are they? Is Pharaoh finally down and out? Let’s find out tomorrow.
questions: There are lots of famous scenes in movies where people give epic speeches that stir up the hearts of people. Can you think of one from a movie that you like? What’s inspiring about it?

Can you ever see yourself being a person who has the courage to stand up before powerful people and warn them of the dangers of disobeying God? Why or why not? What would excite you about that? What would intimidate you?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they had to stand up to someone and warm them to stop doing something wrong. What was it, and how did it go?

thoughts.verse: Do not be deceived: God cannot be mocked. A man reaps what he sows. Whoever sows to please their flesh, from the flesh will reap destruction; whoever sows to please the Spirit, from the Spirit will reap eternal life.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for his faithfulness and that he ultimately fulfills his promises. Ask him to give you courage to step up and lead others to freedom when he calls you.

days.title: Not So Fast
days.scripture: EXODUS 14:5-14 When word reached the king of Egypt that the Israelites had fled, Pharaoh and his officials changed their minds. “What have we done, letting all those Israelite slaves get away?” they asked. So Pharaoh harnessed his chariot and called up his troops. He took with him 600 of Egypt’s best chariots, along with the rest of the chariots of Egypt, each with its commander. The LORD hardened the heart of Pharaoh, the king of Egypt, so he chased after the people of Israel, who had left with fists raised in defiance. The Egyptians chased after them with all the forces in Pharaoh’s army— all his horses and chariots, his charioteers, and his troops. The Egyptians caught up with the people of Israel as they were camped beside the shore near Pi-hahiroth, across from Baal-zephon. As Pharaoh approached, the people of Israel looked up and panicked when they saw the Egyptians overtaking them. They cried out to the LORD, and they said to Moses, “Why did you bring us out here to die in the wilderness? Weren’t there enough graves for us in Egypt? What have you done to us? Why did you make us leave Egypt? Didn’t we tell you this would happen while we were still in Egypt? We said, ‘Leave us alone! Let us be slaves to the Egyptians. It’s better to be a slave in Egypt than a corpse in the wilderness!’” But Moses told the people, “Don’t be afraid. Just stand still and watch the LORD rescue you today. The Egyptians you see today will never be seen again. The LORD himself will fight for you. Just stay calm.”
days.reflection: In almost every movie, there is a famous moment called “The loss of all hope.” This is the highest point of the movie when it feels like things are finally good, the happy ending is here at last, and all will be well. Then, the villain rises again for one final battle and he must be destroyed once and for all. For the children of Israel, this is that moment. Pharaoh just won’t give up. His heart is bent on enslaving the Israelites. He rallies his army and comes after them with all his might. This must have been terrifying for the Israelites. They were in the middle of nowhere, with no way to defend themselves, and they lose their minds. This moment teaches you two very important lessons about becoming a man who can serve others. 1- Understand how people deal with fear 10 As Pharaoh approached, the people of Israel looked up and panicked when they saw the Egyptians overtaking them. Most people don’t handle pressure well; they tend to lose their minds. They panic and freeze up and lose their ability to think clearly. Their survival instincts kick in and they move into reactive mode. This is completely understandable, but not always helpful. This is how the Israelites responded, and you can understand why. An army of chariots is bearing down on them, and they are trapped. They jump into reaction mode, and it makes them forget about the power of God, the miracles they have just witnessed, and the future God has promised. People your age can freak out about all sorts of things. Being rejected by a girl, conflict with friends, end of year exams. It’s natural to feel anxious and afraid in hard moments like this. But you have to learn to lift your eyes beyond the situation, and this requires real maturity. A great starting point is to acknowledge your feelings of fear, name what you are worried about, and begin to process it. This is what Moses did for the people when they were overwhelmed. 2- Courageously point people back to God 13 But Moses told the people, “Don’t be afraid. Just stand still and watch the LORD rescue you today. The Egyptians you see today will never be seen again. The LORD himself will fight for you. Just stay calm.” People your age can sometimes wonder about who or what a leader is. Is it the big guy with the most muscles? The guy with the most money? The most popular guy? The truth is, the leader is the person who can keep calm under pressure. The one who can deal with their own fear, and help others deal with theirs. Moses told the people to stay calm because God was about to show up. You can have the calmness of Moses too. It’s really possible. God has promised to be with you and never leave you. He has promised to help and strengthen you in whatever you face. Jesus said, “I will be with you always.” You have to learn to lift your eyes to his presence, not just focus on the problem. When you can remember that and stay calm, it’s the essence of leadership and the heart of manhood. When you are overwhelmed, take a moment, calm your heart, lift your eyes, and wait for God to act. He showed up for the Israelites; he will show up for you.
questions: How do you normally handle scary situations? Do you tend to freak out, think clearly, or meltdown?
Can you think of a time when someone encouraged and reassured you when you were afraid or struggling? What was the situation, who encouraged you, and how did it make you feel?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they were in a really challenging or scary situation and God comforted them. What was it, and how did God show up?

thoughts.verse: The LORD is my light and my salvation —whom shall I fear? The LORD is the stronghold of my life—of whom shall I be afraid? When the wicked advance against me to devour me, it is my enemies and my foes who will stumble and fall. Though an army besiege me, my heart will not fear; though war break out against me, even then I will be confident.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you from it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you courage in any situation you may be anxious, afraid, and overwhelmed. Ask him to give you the power to encourage others who may be struggling.

days.title: Deliverance And Destruction
days.scripture: EXODUS 14:15-31 Then the LORD said to Moses, “Why are you crying out to me? Tell the people to get moving! Pick up your staff and raise your hand over the sea. Divide the water so the Israelites can walk through the middle of the sea on dry ground. And I will harden the hearts of the Egyptians, and they will charge in after the Israelites. My great glory will be displayed through Pharaoh and his troops, his chariots, and his charioteers. When my glory is displayed through them, all Egypt will see my glory and know that I am the LORD!” Then the angel of God, who had been leading the people of Israel, moved to the rear of the camp. The pillar of cloud also moved from the front and stood behind them. The cloud settled between the Egyptian and Israelite camps. As darkness fell, the cloud turned to fire, lighting up the night. But the Egyptians and Israelites did not approach each other all night. Then Moses raised his hand over the sea, and the LORD opened up a path through the water with a strong east wind. The wind blew all that night, turning the seabed into dry land. So the people of Israel walked through the middle of the sea on dry ground, with walls of water on each side! Then the Egyptians—all of Pharaoh’s horses, chariots, and charioteers —chased them into the middle of the sea. But just before dawn the LORD looked down on the Egyptian army from the pillar of fire and cloud, and he threw their forces into total confusion. He twisted their chariot wheels, making their chariots difficult to drive. “Let’s get out of here—away from these Israelites!” the Egyptians shouted. “The LORD is fighting for them against Egypt!” When all the Israelites had reached the other side, the LORD said to Moses, “Raise your hand over the sea again. Then the waters will rush back and cover the Egyptians and their chariots and charioteers.” So as the sun began to rise, Moses raised his hand over the sea, and the water rushed back into its usual place. The Egyptians tried to escape, but the LORD swept them into the sea. Then the waters returned and covered all the chariots and charioteers—the entire army of Pharaoh. Of all the Egyptians who had chased the Israelites into the sea, not a single one survived. But the people of Israel had walked through the middle of the sea on dry ground, as the water stood up like a wall on both sides. That is how the LORD rescued Israel from the hand of the Egyptians that day. And the Israelites saw the bodies of the Egyptians washed up on the seashore. When the people of Israel saw the mighty power that the LORD had unleashed against the Egyptians, they were filled with awe before him. They put their faith in the LORD and in his servant Moses.
days.reflection: This week, Moses has finally seen his destiny fulfilled. That which he waited 80 years for has finally come to pass. God has acted and his people are free. The Egyptians have drowned, God has been glorified, and the people are delivered. The story of the Exodus is an absolute epic. Plagues, death, gods, nature, fire, power; it's simply incredible. Yet, it happened a long time ago, and it can seem really different than what you face in your world today. You may be reading these stories and thinking to yourself, “Why do I have to learn all this? I am never going to have to handle problems like this. I am never going to face someone like Pharaoh. I am never going to watch plagues destroy false gods. I am never going to lead people freaking out as they escape from slavery. There is no Egypt. There are no Pharaohs. I’m good. Let me get back to my homework and friends. But I wouldn’t be so sure. Don’t be naive. You are living in some wild days. In your lifetime, you have witnessed a pandemic that shut the world down and freaked everyone out. There have been political riots and violence that have shaken cities. You have seen economic problems that have freaked lots of adults out too. Your generation is dealing with the highest levels of anxiety of any generation in recorded history. You may not face the exact same problems Moses did, but you will face real challenges yourself. And God wants to prepare you to be able to handle them. He wants you to have courage when others are full of fear. He wants you to be a man of action when others shrink back. He wants you to be a leader when people need someone to follow. He wants to use you to put his power on display so others trust God. He wants to use you to lead others out of confusion and anxiety into freedom and peace. In your life, you will face tyrants. There will be bullies and enemies that beat people down. You will find people caught in slavery to drugs, sex, and the opinions of others. They will need help getting free. You may be tempted to look away and only worry about yourself, but God will burden your heart to act for others. Jesus is always recruiting men to do amazing things. He recruited fishermen to become apostles, tax collectors to become preachers, and he changed cowards into martyrs. And keep in mind, they were teenagers when he called them. They were way more like you than you can even fathom. Keep moving away from childish selfishness and embracing manhood. Keep shifting from self to others. Keep leaning into what's ahead. That’s where the action is. That’s where God is. That’s where your future is too.
questions: If you could go back in time and witness one of the plagues being poured out on Egypt, which one would it be, and why?
Are there any issues that you see in your world today that you wish God would jump in and fix? What are they? Why?
Have you ever thought of the disciples being teenagers when they were called? How does that change your perspective on what is possible for you?

thoughts.verse: I remain confident of this: I will see the goodness of the LORD in the land of the living. Wait for the LORD; be strong and take heart and wait for the LORD.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you courage to face the issues in your world today. Ask him for peace and power to encourage others who may be wrestling with these things too.

extras: WEEK 6 MANSCHOOL KEEP CALM AND CARRY ON MANSCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: KEEP CALM AND CARRY ON It can be hard to stay calm when others are freaking out. In WW2, the government issued the message to “keep calm and carry on” to help the people deal with the pressure of war. The key to victory was calm under pressure. You can read a bit more here: https://www.london.ac.uk/about- us/history-university-london/story-behind-keep-calm-and-carry Go to the mall, and see if you can find a framed print of this poster. Talk about its history, and then come home and hang it up, or stand it up near where you do your Primal Path times. Then have a discussion about the biggest issues that cause teenagers to freak out today, and brainstorm some ways to respond to them. OPTION 2. WATCH Watch Harriet An amazing true story of Harriet Tubman, who is called a modern-day Moses. An Epic picture of calm under pressure. Inspiring. Also, it’s good to throw a heroine in the mix. https://youtu.be/GqoEs4cG6Uw OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Take your son to his favorite restaurant and recap the week. Check in on how he is doing with the Primal Path so far. Remind him of the “why” that you are doing this. Ask him for ways to make your times together better. See if there is anything in his heart he wants to talk through. Get desserts.
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
